romania.summary: Toți studenții vor avea acces la formare în utilizarea responsabilă a inteligenței artificiale Universitatea extinde curriculumul cu discipline dedicate utilizării AI și automatizării proceselor Inițiativa este susținută prin cercetare, infrastructură… Articolul Universitatea Româno-Americană introduce competențe de inteligență artificială și gândire critică pentru toate specializările academice apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Romgaz a aprobat preluarea combinatului Azomureș. Tranzacția de 69 de milioane de euro intră în faza avizărilor finale

Prin această decizie, producătorul național de gaze naturale preia oficial activitatea operațională a celui mai mare combinat de îngrășăminte chimice …

Gogel Florescu, un şmecher de 44 de ani din cartierul Pantelimon, a mizat pe asta şi a reuşit să-şi anuleze amenda primită după ce a fost prins fără bilet în autobuzul 102. „Simplu. Când am văzut că am de plătit 80 de lei, am pus […] The post Un bărbat prins fără bilet a sunat la STB şi s-a dat drept Trump ca să îşi anuleze amenda appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: În timpul ultimei rearanjări a cutiilor de pantofi, liderii PSD au identificat o nouă linie de atac la adresa lui Ilie Bolojan: să-l acuze pentru toate faptele penale comise de cei care beau pălincă de Bihor. „Bolojan a permis ca în Bihor, timp de foarte mulți ani, să se producă pălincă de Bihor, iar acum […] The post Atac dur la Bolojan după ce un șofer a băut pălincă de Bihor și a făcut un accident appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: CM 2026. Neymar și-a anunțat retragerea de la Naționala Braziliei, după eliminarea în optimile de finală ale Cupei Mondiale
romania.summary: Veteranul Neymar și-a anunțat retragerea din echipa națională după eliminarea Braziliei în optimile de finală ale Cupei Mondiale. „Am încercat, chiar am încercat. Acum s-a terminat”, a declarat jucătorul în vârstă de 34 de ani pentru postul de televiziune Globo după înfrângerea cu 1-2 împotriva Norvegiei. „Am început aici, am terminat aici”, a adăugat acesta, potrivit focus.de.

romania.title: 😂 Șase persoane arestate la ștrandul din Reghin după ce au intrat în bazinul pentru albi
romania.summary: Șase persoane au fost arestate sâmbătă la ștrandul municipal din Reghin după ce au încălcat regulile de acces și au intrat în bazinul rezervat exclusiv pentru albi. Incidentul a avut loc în jurul orei 14:00, când grupul a ignorat semnalizarea clară și indicațiile personalului, provocând un scandal de amploare. Potrivit reprezentanților administrației locale, bazinul în […] The post Șase persoane arestate la ștrandul din Reghin după ce au intrat în bazinul pentru albi appeared first on Times New Roman .

De aceea, propunem întâlniri cu… Articolul Scena Digitală propune în luna iulie două întâlniri cu emoția: Broadway și o poveste despre iubire, familie și acceptare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.

Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Nature-based mental health care is worth the investment, researchers say
medical.summary: A new study underlines the annual AU$7 trillion (US$5 trillion) value of nature in mental health benefits and is calling for urgent government action to expand access to nature as a frontline response to the worsening global mental health crisis.

medical.title: We still misunderstand addiction. Here's what you should know
medical.summary: When most people hear the word addiction, they picture someone whose life has visibly unraveled. Yet addiction is often hidden in plain sight—affecting people in workplaces, schools, sporting clubs and family homes.

medical.title: Researchers develop chimeric allergen receptors as a new immunotherapy approach for asthma
medical.summary: Genetically engineered CAR T cells expressing artificial receptor proteins are increasingly used in the clinic to boost the immune system's response against leukemias and other cancers. In a study published in the Journal of Experimental Medicine (JEM), researchers in Switzerland have adapted this approach to suppress the immune system's response to a common allergen, reducing or preventing asthma symptoms in mice. The technique could eventually be used to treat a wide variety of allergies in humans.

medical.title: Special glove helps people with paralyzed hands grasp objects
medical.summary: The Technical University of Munich (TUM) has developed a soft, pneumatic glove that restores the ability of people with paralyzed hands to grasp objects. To achieve this, researchers at the TUM Chair of Cognitive Systems use electrical signals from the forearm muscles to reliably predict when a person intends to grasp an object. The invention could one day help people whose hands have been paralyzed as a result of accidents or neurological disorders. The research is published in Nature Machine Intelligence.

medical.title: Platinum TALEN enables mass production of engineered cancer-fighting T cells
medical.summary: Hiroshima University researchers have demonstrated a proof of concept for the mass production of genome-edited T cells that can be used to treat malignant tumors, using a genetic engineering technique called Platinum TALEN.

medical.title: Study explores how anxiety turns into deeper stress for dementia caregivers
medical.summary: A new study from Virginia Tech suggests patterns of repetitive negative thinking explain how anxiety develops into deeper stress for caregivers of people living with dementia and that mindfulness training may help identify and interrupt that progression.

medical.title: Editing AI mistakes can cost doctors time when writing to patients
medical.summary: Errors and irrelevant details mean physicians may spend more time editing AI-drafted responses than it would take to write them, a large study of an online patient portal shows. Artificial intelligence is spreading rapidly in health care, with the goal of streamlining critical but onerous clerical tasks such as note-taking and charting so physicians and nurses can devote more time to patients.

medical.title: Novel biomarker research offers new hope in the fight against colorectal cancer
medical.summary: Emerging discoveries in liquid biomarker research promise to transform the early detection, diagnosis and treatment monitoring of colorectal cancer (CRC), according to a new study, offering less invasive alternatives to traditional methods that often require surgical interventions or tissue biopsies.

medical.title: Video games might modestly sharpen your memory and other cognitive skills, review suggests
medical.summary: Because video games are a regular part of many people's everyday lives, researchers have spent a lot of time trying to determine whether they are beneficial or detrimental to brain health. A new study, published in Acta Psychologica, has compiled 20 years of research on how video games affect cognitive abilities into a single systematic review and meta-analysis. This comprehensive study indicates that video games may provide some helpful cognitive benefits to gamers.

medical.title: The workers who 'just get it': How lived experience strengthens addiction recovery
medical.summary: People with lived experience of alcohol and drug use are helping others navigate recovery. New UNSW research shows their experience is a powerful form of expertise when workplaces recognize and support it. The research is published in the International Journal of Drug Policy.

medical.title: New framework could help unlock the power of belief in health care
medical.summary: A doctor's confidence, the design of a clinic and even the language used to explain a treatment may shape a patient's belief in their care, according to new QUT research published in the Journal of Psychosomatic Research.

medical.title: PET scans reveal stage-linked tau signal in Huntington's disease brains
medical.summary: A study conducted by the Sant Pau Research Institute (IR Sant Pau) and Hospital de Sant Pau has identified for the first time in living individuals a brain pattern related to the tau protein that changes according to the stage of Huntington's disease. This discovery opens the door both to the use of new biomarkers for monitoring the disease and to the development of treatments for a condition for which no therapeutic options are currently available.

medical.title: Learning languages could net you a younger brain, study says
medical.summary: Is your teenager thinking about taking a Spanish or French class? Tell them there's a hidden benefit to picking up another language—their brain might age more slowly, a new study says.

medical.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
medical.summary: A long-term Yale study is challenging one of the biggest myths about aging. Nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time, despite the common belief that aging means constant decline. Researchers found that people with more positive attitudes about getting older were significantly more likely to show these gains.

medical.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
medical.summary: As newborn neurons make their way through the developing brain, they must squeeze through incredibly tight spaces to reach their final destinations. Researchers discovered that this physical journey routinely causes some of the most severe forms of DNA damage—double-strand breaks—yet the young brain has evolved an impressive ability to repair the damage almost immediately.

medical.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
medical.summary: Researchers have concluded that nicotine vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancers, based on evidence ranging from human biomarkers to animal and laboratory studies. The findings challenge the idea that vaping is a harmless alternative to smoking and suggest health risks may be emerging much sooner than many expected.

science.title: Webb uncovers dust-shrouded heart of Centaurus A after galaxy clash 2 billion years ago
science.summary: In new images from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope to celebrate its fourth science anniversary, a familiar galaxy transforms into something far richer and far more complex than ever seen before. Webb's unprecedented sensitivity across near- and mid-infrared wavelengths cuts through the thick lanes of dust that obscure Centaurus A's center in visible light, showing a densely packed tapestry of individual stars and an active, ever-changing galaxy. These images mark four years of better-than-anticipated performance and successful science operations for the most powerful space telescope in history.

science.title: We assume students see pictures in their minds as they learn. But not everyone can
science.summary: Picture a bright red apple. Most people can do this easily. They imagine the apple's shape, color and shine. But for others, the image is vague and blurry or they "see" nothing at all. This is known as aphantasia—a "blind mind's eye."

science.title: Scientists discover smart way to supercharge soft robotics and better support rehabilitation patients
science.summary: Researchers have found an ingenious way to make soft robots and wearable technology more than three times more powerful by harnessing the surface tension of a tiny liquid metal droplet smaller than a raindrop.

science.title: Upside‑down whales aren't sick or hurt—they're just resting
science.summary: If you stand on one of Australia's southern shorelines at this time of year, you might be lucky enough to spot a southern right whale (Eubalaena australis) swimming in the shallow waters. These ocean giants have migrated from the Southern Ocean and Antarctica to breed, give birth and rest. If you're really lucky, you may even see one of them resting upside down.

science.title: The evolutionary pressure behind sexual asymmetry revealed in yeast cell study
science.summary: A major transition in evolution is the shift from asexual to sexual reproduction in early organisms. But why would a yeast cell, which usually reproduces asexually, choose to mate with a very different partner in times of stress?

science.title: NASA Webb Uncovers Unusual Galaxy Shaped by Cosmic Collision
science.summary: In new images from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope to celebrate its fourth science anniversary, a familiar galaxy transforms into something far richer, and far more complex, than ever seen before. Webb’s unprecedented sensitivity across near- and mid-infrared wavelengths cuts through the thick lanes of dust that obscure Centaurus A’s center in visible light, showing […]

science.title: Scientists observe water's behavior in a single molecular layer
science.summary: New research has revealed that water behaves differently when confined to spaces just one molecule thick. For the first time, scientists have directly measured the vibrational signatures of truly two-dimensional water. In a study published in Nature Communications, researchers used ultrathin channels only a few angstroms high to trap water in isolated layers and probe how its hydrogen-bonding network changes under extreme confinement.

science.title: Chemists capture structure of the elusive borylnitrene trapped in a crystal using X-ray
science.summary: Nitrenes are the ghosts of synthetic chemistry, formed in an instant and gone just as quickly, rearranging into something entirely different. These highly reactive intermediates are widely used in synthesis, yet remain notoriously difficult to study because they rapidly transform into more stable structures through a process called 1,2-migration.

science.title: Atomic-level engineering of Cu nanoclusters improves conversion of CO₂ to fuel
science.summary: Researchers at Tohoku University have discovered a promising strategy that converts harmful carbon dioxide into valuable fuels and chemicals by precisely altering nanoclusters made of copper.

science.title: New workflow tool gives scientists a clearer view of how DNA is regulated
science.summary: Researchers from the Cancer Science Institute of Singapore (CSI Singapore) at NUS have developed a new method that allows scientists to better understand how DNA is organized and regulated inside cells. The study, published in the journal Nature Communications on May 26, 2026, introduces a method called qChIP-MS, which enables researchers to identify groups of proteins that work together at specific locations on DNA.

science.title: From birdsong to galaxies: Unleashing the potential of citizen science
science.summary: Everyone could contribute dramatically more to scientific knowledge if given better recognition, administrative support and funding stability, new research has found. An international collaboration of 16 organizations used surveys and group discussions to identify key challenges, opportunities and best practices in citizen science projects.

science.title: AI maps 991 aromatic plants, identifies scent compounds that may improve sleep
science.summary: Food scientists at the National University of Singapore (NUS) have used machine learning to sift through more than 2,300 scent molecules from aromatic plants and identify compounds with sleep-promoting potential, an approach that could accelerate the discovery of natural sleep aids.

science.title: Earth's deepest rocks help define upper limit for viscosity beyond which materials effectively become rigid
science.summary: Viscosity is one of the most fundamental physical properties used to describe how materials flow. It governs the movement of liquids, molten rocks and even slowly deforming regions deep inside the Earth. While scientists have long studied materials with low or moderate viscosities, a simple but important question has remained largely unexplored: Is there a physically meaningful upper limit to viscosity?

science.title: Older than the sun: Astronomers find new clues to the origin of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS
science.summary: Astronomers have used the European Southern Observatory's Very Large Telescope (ESO's VLT) to study the composition of 3I/ATLAS, the brightest interstellar object ever seen, in detail. By measuring specific chemical fingerprints—the first observations of this kind for a comet that formed outside the solar system—they found that 3I/ATLAS likely originated in the outskirts of an old star system.

science.title: Emotional ups and downs boost engagement but reduce sales in livestream influencer selling
science.summary: Influencers who frequently switch between emotions during livestreams may attract more likes and comments, but they are likely to sell fewer products, according to new research from QUT. Published in the Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science, the research analyzed more than 12,900 product pitches and 80,600 minute-by-minute observations across 1,308 livestream selling sessions.

science.title: A new approach to measuring sheep liveweight
science.summary: Sheep farmers could soon be able to estimate their flock's live weight and fleece weight in real time, without the need for labor-intensive handling. The new system, being developed by CSIRO, Australia's national science agency, will capture measurements in real time as animals move naturally through their environment. FlockMate is a two-year research project.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

Incidentul a avut loc în jurul orei 14:00, când grupul a ignorat semnalizarea clară și indicațiile personalului, provocând un scandal de amploare. mix_items.title: CM 2026. Neymar și-a anunțat retragerea de la Naționala Braziliei, după eliminarea în optimile de finală ale Cupei Mondiale
mix_items.summary: Veteranul Neymar și-a anunțat retragerea din echipa națională după eliminarea Braziliei în optimile de finală ale Cupei Mondiale. „Am încercat, chiar am încercat. Acum s-a terminat”, a declarat jucătorul în vârstă de 34 de ani pentru postul de televiziune Globo după înfrângerea cu 1-2 împotriva Norvegiei. „Am început aici, am terminat aici”, a adăugat acesta, potrivit focus.de.

mix_items.title: Webb uncovers dust-shrouded heart of Centaurus A after galaxy clash 2 billion years ago
mix_items.summary: In new images from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope to celebrate its fourth science anniversary, a familiar galaxy transforms into something far richer and far more complex than ever seen before. Webb's unprecedented sensitivity across near- and mid-infrared wavelengths cuts through the thick lanes of dust that obscure Centaurus A's center in visible light, showing a densely packed tapestry of individual stars and an active, ever-changing galaxy. These images mark four years of better-than-anticipated performance and successful science operations for the most powerful space telescope in history.

mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: We assume students see pictures in their minds as they learn. But not everyone can
mix_items.summary: Picture a bright red apple. Most people can do this easily. They imagine the apple's shape, color and shine. But for others, the image is vague and blurry or they "see" nothing at all. This is known as aphantasia—a "blind mind's eye."

mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Nature-based mental health care is worth the investment, researchers say
mix_items.summary: A new study underlines the annual AU$7 trillion (US$5 trillion) value of nature in mental health benefits and is calling for urgent government action to expand access to nature as a frontline response to the worsening global mental health crisis.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover smart way to supercharge soft robotics and better support rehabilitation patients
mix_items.summary: Researchers have found an ingenious way to make soft robots and wearable technology more than three times more powerful by harnessing the surface tension of a tiny liquid metal droplet smaller than a raindrop.

mix_items.title: We still misunderstand addiction. Here's what you should know
mix_items.summary: When most people hear the word addiction, they picture someone whose life has visibly unraveled. Yet addiction is often hidden in plain sight—affecting people in workplaces, schools, sporting clubs and family homes.

mix_items.title: Upside‑down whales aren't sick or hurt—they're just resting
mix_items.summary: If you stand on one of Australia's southern shorelines at this time of year, you might be lucky enough to spot a southern right whale (Eubalaena australis) swimming in the shallow waters. These ocean giants have migrated from the Southern Ocean and Antarctica to breed, give birth and rest. If you're really lucky, you may even see one of them resting upside down.

mix_items.title: The evolutionary pressure behind sexual asymmetry revealed in yeast cell study
mix_items.summary: A major transition in evolution is the shift from asexual to sexual reproduction in early organisms. But why would a yeast cell, which usually reproduces asexually, choose to mate with a very different partner in times of stress?

mix_items.title: Researchers develop chimeric allergen receptors as a new immunotherapy approach for asthma
mix_items.summary: Genetically engineered CAR T cells expressing artificial receptor proteins are increasingly used in the clinic to boost the immune system's response against leukemias and other cancers. In a study published in the Journal of Experimental Medicine (JEM), researchers in Switzerland have adapted this approach to suppress the immune system's response to a common allergen, reducing or preventing asthma symptoms in mice. The technique could eventually be used to treat a wide variety of allergies in humans.

mix_items.title: Special glove helps people with paralyzed hands grasp objects
mix_items.summary: The Technical University of Munich (TUM) has developed a soft, pneumatic glove that restores the ability of people with paralyzed hands to grasp objects. To achieve this, researchers at the TUM Chair of Cognitive Systems use electrical signals from the forearm muscles to reliably predict when a person intends to grasp an object. The invention could one day help people whose hands have been paralyzed as a result of accidents or neurological disorders. The research is published in Nature Machine Intelligence.

mix_items.title: NASA Webb Uncovers Unusual Galaxy Shaped by Cosmic Collision
mix_items.summary: In new images from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope to celebrate its fourth science anniversary, a familiar galaxy transforms into something far richer, and far more complex, than ever seen before. Webb’s unprecedented sensitivity across near- and mid-infrared wavelengths cuts through the thick lanes of dust that obscure Centaurus A’s center in visible light, showing […]

mix_items.title: Scientists observe water's behavior in a single molecular layer
mix_items.summary: New research has revealed that water behaves differently when confined to spaces just one molecule thick. For the first time, scientists have directly measured the vibrational signatures of truly two-dimensional water. In a study published in Nature Communications, researchers used ultrathin channels only a few angstroms high to trap water in isolated layers and probe how its hydrogen-bonding network changes under extreme confinement.

mix_items.title: Chemists capture structure of the elusive borylnitrene trapped in a crystal using X-ray
mix_items.summary: Nitrenes are the ghosts of synthetic chemistry, formed in an instant and gone just as quickly, rearranging into something entirely different. These highly reactive intermediates are widely used in synthesis, yet remain notoriously difficult to study because they rapidly transform into more stable structures through a process called 1,2-migration.

mix_items.title: Platinum TALEN enables mass production of engineered cancer-fighting T cells
mix_items.summary: Hiroshima University researchers have demonstrated a proof of concept for the mass production of genome-edited T cells that can be used to treat malignant tumors, using a genetic engineering technique called Platinum TALEN.

mix_items.title: Study explores how anxiety turns into deeper stress for dementia caregivers
mix_items.summary: A new study from Virginia Tech suggests patterns of repetitive negative thinking explain how anxiety develops into deeper stress for caregivers of people living with dementia and that mindfulness training may help identify and interrupt that progression.

mix_items.title: Editing AI mistakes can cost doctors time when writing to patients
mix_items.summary: Errors and irrelevant details mean physicians may spend more time editing AI-drafted responses than it would take to write them, a large study of an online patient portal shows. Artificial intelligence is spreading rapidly in health care, with the goal of streamlining critical but onerous clerical tasks such as note-taking and charting so physicians and nurses can devote more time to patients.

mix_items.title: Atomic-level engineering of Cu nanoclusters improves conversion of CO₂ to fuel
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Tohoku University have discovered a promising strategy that converts harmful carbon dioxide into valuable fuels and chemicals by precisely altering nanoclusters made of copper.

mix_items.title: New workflow tool gives scientists a clearer view of how DNA is regulated
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the Cancer Science Institute of Singapore (CSI Singapore) at NUS have developed a new method that allows scientists to better understand how DNA is organized and regulated inside cells. The study, published in the journal Nature Communications on May 26, 2026, introduces a method called qChIP-MS, which enables researchers to identify groups of proteins that work together at specific locations on DNA.

mix_items.title: From birdsong to galaxies: Unleashing the potential of citizen science
mix_items.summary: Everyone could contribute dramatically more to scientific knowledge if given better recognition, administrative support and funding stability, new research has found. An international collaboration of 16 organizations used surveys and group discussions to identify key challenges, opportunities and best practices in citizen science projects.

mix_items.title: Novel biomarker research offers new hope in the fight against colorectal cancer
mix_items.summary: Emerging discoveries in liquid biomarker research promise to transform the early detection, diagnosis and treatment monitoring of colorectal cancer (CRC), according to a new study, offering less invasive alternatives to traditional methods that often require surgical interventions or tissue biopsies.

mix_items.title: Video games might modestly sharpen your memory and other cognitive skills, review suggests
mix_items.summary: Because video games are a regular part of many people's everyday lives, researchers have spent a lot of time trying to determine whether they are beneficial or detrimental to brain health. A new study, published in Acta Psychologica, has compiled 20 years of research on how video games affect cognitive abilities into a single systematic review and meta-analysis. This comprehensive study indicates that video games may provide some helpful cognitive benefits to gamers.

mix_items.title: AI maps 991 aromatic plants, identifies scent compounds that may improve sleep
mix_items.summary: Food scientists at the National University of Singapore (NUS) have used machine learning to sift through more than 2,300 scent molecules from aromatic plants and identify compounds with sleep-promoting potential, an approach that could accelerate the discovery of natural sleep aids.

mix_items.title: Earth's deepest rocks help define upper limit for viscosity beyond which materials effectively become rigid
mix_items.summary: Viscosity is one of the most fundamental physical properties used to describe how materials flow. It governs the movement of liquids, molten rocks and even slowly deforming regions deep inside the Earth. While scientists have long studied materials with low or moderate viscosities, a simple but important question has remained largely unexplored: Is there a physically meaningful upper limit to viscosity?

mix_items.title: The workers who 'just get it': How lived experience strengthens addiction recovery
mix_items.summary: People with lived experience of alcohol and drug use are helping others navigate recovery. New UNSW research shows their experience is a powerful form of expertise when workplaces recognize and support it. The research is published in the International Journal of Drug Policy.

mix_items.title: Older than the sun: Astronomers find new clues to the origin of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have used the European Southern Observatory's Very Large Telescope (ESO's VLT) to study the composition of 3I/ATLAS, the brightest interstellar object ever seen, in detail. By measuring specific chemical fingerprints—the first observations of this kind for a comet that formed outside the solar system—they found that 3I/ATLAS likely originated in the outskirts of an old star system.

mix_items.title: New framework could help unlock the power of belief in health care
mix_items.summary: A doctor's confidence, the design of a clinic and even the language used to explain a treatment may shape a patient's belief in their care, according to new QUT research published in the Journal of Psychosomatic Research.

mix_items.title: Emotional ups and downs boost engagement but reduce sales in livestream influencer selling
mix_items.summary: Influencers who frequently switch between emotions during livestreams may attract more likes and comments, but they are likely to sell fewer products, according to new research from QUT. Published in the Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science, the research analyzed more than 12,900 product pitches and 80,600 minute-by-minute observations across 1,308 livestream selling sessions.

mix_items.title: PET scans reveal stage-linked tau signal in Huntington's disease brains
mix_items.summary: A study conducted by the Sant Pau Research Institute (IR Sant Pau) and Hospital de Sant Pau has identified for the first time in living individuals a brain pattern related to the tau protein that changes according to the stage of Huntington's disease. This discovery opens the door both to the use of new biomarkers for monitoring the disease and to the development of treatments for a condition for which no therapeutic options are currently available.

mix_items.title: A new approach to measuring sheep liveweight
mix_items.summary: Sheep farmers could soon be able to estimate their flock's live weight and fleece weight in real time, without the need for labor-intensive handling. The new system, being developed by CSIRO, Australia's national science agency, will capture measurements in real time as animals move naturally through their environment. FlockMate is a two-year research project.

mix_items.title: Learning languages could net you a younger brain, study says
mix_items.summary: Is your teenager thinking about taking a Spanish or French class? Tell them there's a hidden benefit to picking up another language—their brain might age more slowly, a new study says.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
mix_items.summary: A long-term Yale study is challenging one of the biggest myths about aging. Nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time, despite the common belief that aging means constant decline. Researchers found that people with more positive attitudes about getting older were significantly more likely to show these gains.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
mix_items.summary: As newborn neurons make their way through the developing brain, they must squeeze through incredibly tight spaces to reach their final destinations. Researchers discovered that this physical journey routinely causes some of the most severe forms of DNA damage—double-strand breaks—yet the young brain has evolved an impressive ability to repair the damage almost immediately.

mix_items.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers have concluded that nicotine vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancers, based on evidence ranging from human biomarkers to animal and laboratory studies. The findings challenge the idea that vaping is a harmless alternative to smoking and suggest health risks may be emerging much sooner than many expected.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am vrut sa dorm devreme, dar gandurile aveau program prelungit.

satire_ro.title: Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis
